analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 4 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Inconclusive – the architecture appears correct, but the internal dmi_jtag implementation must be audited.
findings.vulnerability_category: set/clear jtag_unlock / Peripheral lock state via reglk_wrapper / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: dmi_jtag instantiation with jtag_hash_i, okey_hash_i, ikey_hash_i, we_flag, and jtag_unlock_o.
reglk_wrapper uses jtag_unlock in reset condition: if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)) reglk_mem <= 0.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG unlock logic Operation: set/clear jtag_unlock Object: Peripheral lock state via reglk_wrapper Expected guard: jtag_unlock asserted only after successful hash comparison, and no other source can drive it. Observed behavior: jtag_unlock is generated exclusively by the dmi_jtag module, which receives jtag_hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash, and we_flag_4. No other RTL in riscv_peripherals assigns jtag_unlock. reglk_wrapper uses jtag_unlock as a reset condition for its lock memory. Path: dmi_jtag (internal logic) → jtag_unlock → reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock → reglk_mem reset. Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If dmi_jtag implements a robust hash comparison, the unlock is secure; if bypassable, locks can be cleared.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No visibility into dmi_jtag internals; cannot confirm the hash comparison logic or whether testmode_i/debug_mode_i influence jtag_unlock.

findings.summary: Inconclusive – the architecture shows potential for correct guarding, but internal implementation needs review.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / Fuse data / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: i_pkt_wrapper instantiation with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i connections; fuse interface ports.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Fuse read interface Operation: read Object: Fuse data Expected guard: Fuse reads must be gated by privilege/lock; only trusted initiators should be allowed. Observed behavior: fuse_req, fuse_addr, fuse_rdata are generated inside pkt_wrapper, which receives access-control inputs reglk_ctrl_i (from reglk array) and acct_ctrl_i (acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5]). The wrapper likely enforces some form of access control, but its internal implementation is not visible. Path: pkt_wrapper → fuse_req/addr → pkt internal logic → fuse_rdata. Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If pkt_wrapper fails to block unauthorized reads, secret fuse data could leak.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: pkt_wrapper source code not provided; cannot verify how fuse read requests are gated.

findings.summary: Candidate violation: we_flag signals can override lock and access-control on AES0 and potentially other peripherals. The security depends entirely on these external signals being driven securely.
findings.vulnerability_category: override write protection / Access-control and lock states / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: AES0 reglk_ctrl_i = reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 – we_flag_1 can force the lock byte to enable writes.
acc_ctrl_o[7:0] = acct_mem[0] | {8{we_flag}} – we_flag forces write-enable for peripheral 0.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Write-enable flags (we_flag_0..we_flag_4) Operation: override write protection Object: Access-control and lock states Expected guard: we_flag must only be asserted under strict, secure conditions; not by unprivileged masters. Observed behavior: we_flag_1 is ORed into the reglk_ctrl byte for AES0, effectively overriding its lock bit. In acct_wrapper, we_flag is ORed into the lowest access-control output bit, forcing write-enable for one peripheral slot. The source of we_flag signals is external to riscv_peripherals and not visible in the provided RTL. Path: External we_flag_1 → OR with reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] → aes0_wrapper; external we_flag → acct_wrapper.acc_ctrl_o[7:0] forced high. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Potential bypass of lock protection, depending on outside control of we_flag pins.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No visibility into the source of we_flag signals; they are external inputs to the chip/macro.

findings.summary: Confirmed violation: CLINT and PLIC are not gated by privilege or lock, contradicting the expected guard.
findings.vulnerability_category: inconsistent application to peripheral access / CLINT and PLIC peripherals / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: i_clint instantiation has no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i connections; only testmode_i.
i_plic instantiation has no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i connections.
Example of protected peripheral: aes0_wrapper receives acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1].

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Privilege level (priv_lvl_i) Operation: inconsistent application to peripheral access Object: CLINT and PLIC peripherals Expected guard: CLINT and PLIC (which contain privilege-sensitive CSRs) must be protected by priv_lvl_i and acc_ctrl_c. Observed behavior: CLINT instantiation (i_clint) receives only testmode_i; no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i. PLIC instantiation similarly lacks access-control inputs. Other peripherals (AES, SHA, HMAC, Reglk, etc.) all receive acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i] inputs. This means CLINT and PLIC are accessible without privilege or lock checks. Path: AXI request → clint/plic wrapper → no privilege check → internal registers. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Violation: machine-mode timer/interrupt registers exposed to unprivileged software, allowing denial-of-service or privilege escalation.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
